content:
The dangerous number is not steady-state usage. It is how many memory pages change while a background child is alive.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Redis can sit comfortably below its memory limit and still become an OOM candidate during persistence.
The dashboard is not necessarily wrong. It is answering the wrong question.
Normal memory usage describes the parent process. An RDB save or AOF rewrite introduces a child process, copy-on-write pages, extra buffers, disk pressure, and a period when the operating system must support more than the quiet baseline.
Redis documentation says a write-heavy instance can use up to twice its normal memory while saving an RDB file or rewriting AOF.
That is not a promise that every fork doubles resident memory. It is the capacity-planning boundary people ignore because copy-on-write sounds free.
It is only free while pages stay unchanged.
Fork Does Not Copy Everything Immediately
Redis uses fork() for background persistence work. The parent continues serving clients while the child writes a point-in-time representation to disk.
At fork time, Linux does not eagerly duplicate every byte of the Redis dataset. Parent and child initially map the same physical memory pages. Those pages are marked for copy-on-write.
The useful mental model is:

• Unchanged page: parent and child keep sharing it.

• Changed page: Linux preserves the child’s old view and gives the writer a private copy.

• Child exits: its mappings and private copies can be released.
So a 20 GB dataset does not instantly consume another 20 GB of physical RAM just because Redis forked. But the kernel must be able to honor the possible copies created while the child runs.
The amount copied depends on pages dirtied during that window, not merely the number of commands.
A small logical update can touch allocator metadata or a page containing other objects. Repeated changes to the same already-copied page do not keep making one fresh copy per command, while writes spread across many pages can produce much more copy-on-write memory.
This is why write distribution matters. A rough “writes per second” graph does not reveal how broadly those writes touch memory.
The child lifetime matters too. Slow storage keeps the snapshot or rewrite alive longer. A longer window gives the parent more time to dirty more pages.
You write it, Redis forks it, the kernel accounts for it, and the disk decides how long everyone suffers.
The Memory Graph Hides the Peak You Need
Teams often alert on used_memory, then assume the gap between that value and host RAM is usable headroom.
That comparison mixes several different views.
Redis INFO memory exposes data such as used_memory, used_memory_rss, allocator statistics, fragmentation ratios, and peak values. The operating system sees process mappings, shared pages, resident pages, page cache, other services, and swap. A container adds cgroup limits that may be lower than host capacity.
No single number answers every question.
Start with these:
redis-cli INFO memory
redis-cli INFO persistence
redis-cli INFO statsDuring a background operation, inspect:

• rdb_bgsave_in_progress

• aof_rewrite_in_progress

• latest_fork_usec

• rdb_last_bgsave_status

• aof_last_bgrewrite_status

• used_memory_rss

• mem_fragmentation_ratio
On Linux, add the process and cgroup view:
ps -o pid,ppid,rss,vsz,stat,cmd -C redis-server
cat /proc/$(pidof redis-server)/smaps_rollup
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.current
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.eventsPaths vary with the cgroup layout and runtime, but the distinction does not: Redis’s configured maxmemory is not a cap on total process RSS or total cgroup memory.
maxmemory governs Redis’s dataset-related eviction behavior. It does not reserve room for fork overhead, allocator fragmentation, persistence buffers, client output buffers, replication buffers, kernel memory, or another process.
A graph sampled every minute can also miss a short peak. If the OOM killer acts before the next scrape, the chart may show a healthy point before the kill and a restarted process afterward.
Use higher-resolution telemetry around persistence events. Correlate memory, child-process state, fork duration, disk latency, write rate, evictions, and cgroup OOM counters.
Overcommit Fixes Fork Admission, Not Capacity
Redis recommends setting Linux vm.overcommit_memory to 1.
The reason is specific. Before creating the child, Linux has to decide whether the allocation can be supported. With conservative overcommit behavior, fork() can fail because the theoretical private memory requirement is large, even when copy-on-write means most pages will remain shared.
Check the current value:
sysctl vm.overcommit_memorySet it persistently through the operating system’s normal sysctl configuration:
vm.overcommit_memory = 1Then apply it using your distribution’s supported process.
This setting allows optimistic allocation. It does not create RAM.
That distinction is operationally important:
→ vm.overcommit_memory=1 can prevent an unnecessarily rejected fork.
→ It cannot prevent physical memory exhaustion when the workload actually dirties enough pages.
→ It cannot override a container memory limit.
→ It does not make swap latency harmless.
Redis also recommends swap as a guardrail on Linux, because it can turn an immediate kill into detectable pressure. But Redis serving hot data from swap can produce severe latency. Swap is breathing room for response, not a substitute for capacity.
If background saving fails, inspect both Redis persistence status and logs. If the process is killed, inspect kernel or cgroup evidence rather than guessing:
journalctl -k --since "-30 min" | grep -Ei 'oom|killed process'
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.eventsThe key is to separate three failures:

• The kernel refuses fork().

• The fork succeeds, then real memory pressure grows.

• Persistence survives, but latency deteriorates because memory or storage is pressured.
One sysctl does not solve all three.
Size for the Dirty-Page Window
Capacity planning should start with a peak envelope, not a comfortable average.
At minimum, budget for:

• Redis steady-state resident memory

• Copy-on-write growth during the longest expected child lifetime

• Allocator fragmentation

• AOF rewrite and replication buffers where applicable

• Client output buffers

• Operating-system and sidecar memory

• A deliberate safety margin
Do not turn “up to 2x” into a universal sizing formula. Measure your workload.
Run a persistence operation in a production-like environment with the expected dataset, write pattern, storage class, persistence settings, replication behavior, and cgroup limit. Watch peak cgroup memory and used_memory_rss from before the fork until after the child exits.
Repeat under a write-heavy period. Test the slow-storage case, because child duration controls the exposure window.
Useful questions:

• How long does BGSAVE or BGREWRITEAOF remain active?

• How much does RSS rise during that period?

• Are writes concentrated or spread across the dataset?

• Does storage latency extend the operation?

• Does the cgroup have room above the measured peak?

• Does an automated AOF rewrite begin at a predictable threshold?

• Can replication trigger an RDB save even when local persistence assumptions say otherwise?
Redis’s administration documentation notes that replication can cause RDB saves even if persistence is disabled, except in cases such as diskless replication. “We turned snapshots off” is therefore not enough evidence that the primary will never fork for this work.
Schedule and configuration can reduce risk. Faster storage shortens child lifetime. Controlling rewrite timing can avoid the busiest write window. Diskless replication may change the full-sync path. Sharding reduces the memory at risk per process, though it adds distributed-system complexity.
None of these removes the need for headroom.
Treat Persistence as a Capacity Event
The first response to a Redis memory alert should identify whether a child operation is active, whether RSS is rising, and whether the host or cgroup is approaching its real limit.
A compact triage sequence:
redis-cli INFO persistence
redis-cli INFO memory
redis-cli INFO replication
ps -o pid,ppid,rss,vsz,stat,etime,cmd -C redis-server
cat /proc/pressure/memory
journalctl -k --since "-15 min" | grep -Ei 'oom|killed process'Do not reflexively kill the child. Understand which durability or replication operation it is performing and what aborting it means. Do not raise the container limit without checking node capacity. Do not disable persistence during an incident without understanding the recovery-point trade-off.
For a command-first diagnosis tree covering Redis OOM, persistence, replication, and data-loss failure modes, use the Redis War Room. Copy-paste commands. No fluff.
The durable fix is an explicit peak-memory budget and alerts tied to fork activity. Alert on failed background saves, failed rewrites, long fork times, rising RSS, cgroup pressure, and shrinking headroom. Test those alerts while a child is actually running.
Redis memory does not become mysterious during fork(). The accounting simply changes.
Steady state tells you what Redis costs when nothing unusual is happening. Persistence is the moment that proves whether the machine was sized for Redis at all.
Want to test whether you can recognize the next failure from symptoms instead of syntax? Froquiz has 10,000+ questions plus scenario-based Senior Dev Challenges across Redis-adjacent topics such as Linux, SQL, Docker, AWS, and microservices.
